question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed two well-timed didactic choices: brute force vs. hash map (Turn 1), and two-pass vs. single-pass insertion (Turn 2). For an easy problem with limited decision points, this hits most of the meaningful forks.

question_quality.rationale: Both choices target the actual crux: the time/space tradeoff is the canonical lesson of Two Sum, and the single-pass vs. two-pass framing surfaces the subtle duplicate-index gotcha that makes the lazy-build pattern click.

scaffolding.rationale: Progression is clean: first pick the algorithm class, then pick the implementation ordering once committed. No spoilers — the duplicate-index issue is embedded in the diff via the `!= i` guard rather than spelled out, letting the user discover the rationale.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Replies are concise, validate the user's reasoning ("Nailed it"), and connect the pattern to broader problems (subarray sums, pair differences). Back-and-forth feels natural and the user's final summary shows the lesson landed.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code is correct, passes tests covering normal, offset, and duplicate cases — equivalent to the baseline's solution. Slightly more compact than baseline (no docstring/comments) but functionally identical.

friction.rationale: 73s vs baseline's 69s and 3 turns vs 2 — essentially the same length, and the extra turn buys two genuine pedagogical decisions including the non-obvious insertion-order question. Proportional payoff for negligible extra cost.
